summary: DHH reflects on his diminishing ability to work while listening to vocal music, noting that lyrics increasingly demand his attention rather than staying in the background. He describes resorting to instrumental music like the Braveheart theme on repeat, then experimenting with different vocal music to find what doesn't distract him. He finds the results surprisingly inconsistent - rap music works fine while simpler vocals derail his focus entirely.
key_points: His ability to work with background music has deteriorated over time, with vocals becoming increasingly distracting
Instrumental music works reliably but gets boring on repeat
The relationship between lyrical complexity and distraction level appears counterintuitive and almost random
Rap with continuous vocals can be less distracting than simpler, sparser lyrics
money_quotes: The task of listening to the lyrics seems to be getting discontent with the status of "non-essential" and keeps upping itself to "important".
I used to be able to work or read with just about any type of music playing in the background, but during the last year or so that ability have been fading rapidly.
The 1:42 minute Braveheart theme has consistently proven effective and has found its way into the repeat-of-the-day section a lot, but it does get somewhat boring after a while.
I've been listening to "Let Me Blow Ya Mind" by Eve (featuring Gwen) all day along with no problems whatsoever even though it has Eve rapping continuesly through the song.
My level of succes seems almost random.
key_insight: The distractiveness of music while working has little to do with lyrical complexity and more to do with some harder-to-pin-down quality of how music engages attention.
